title: Indexing
objectives: Choose the right index for a workload
Understand covering, partial and composite indexes
Avoid index bloat and write amplification

sections.markdown: An index is a separate data structure (usually a B+ tree) that maps lookup keys to row positions, turning O(n) table scans into O(log n) lookups — at the cost of extra storage and slower writes.

The **leftmost-prefix rule**: a composite index on (A, B, C) serves queries filtering by A, or A+B, or A+B+C — but NOT queries filtering by B alone. Design compound indexes to match your top queries in that order.
Indexing every column 'just in case.' Each index slows every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and eats storage. Measure query patterns first; add indexes to real slow queries.
Use EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm the index is used (index scan, not seq scan). Watch for **index bloat** in Postgres; rebuild/repack when needed.

flashcards.back: A composite index (A,B,C) serves queries on A, A+B, A+B+C — not B alone or C alone.
One that includes all columns a query needs, letting the DB answer without reading the row (index-only scan).
Every index slows writes and eats storage; add indexes to real hot queries.

quiz.q: An index on (user_id, status) does NOT help…
quiz.explain: Leftmost prefix — status alone can't use the index.
All needed columns are in the index.
Index only the rows matching a predicate.

challenges.text: Add a single-column B-tree; prove the plan uses it with EXPLAIN.
Add a composite matching a top query; test with/without.
Convert a slow query into an index-only scan with a covering index.
Detect bloat and rebuild/repack indexes without downtime.

interviewQuestions: How does a B-tree index work?
Why is indexing every column bad?
